scores.note: It isolates the load-bearing claim cleanly: Euclid's escape from any finite list, not a tour of the page's proof catalog.
The composite-q branch and the generator overread are present, but the film mostly names those limits instead of walking the boundary failure the notes identify.
It reenacts the proof, but it never makes the notes' evidence hierarchy visible, so what the page demonstrates and what it merely catalogs blur together.
The numbers scene leads with the 200-proof catalog fact the notes explicitly demote, while skipping the factorial bridge that would use quantity to sharpen Euclid's actual scope.
It names the real alternative and costs the choice honestly: Euclid buys elementary mechanism by giving up generation, location, and density.
The close rules favorably but narrowly, names the overclaim risk, and tells the viewer what skepticism to carry forward.

critiques.problem: "The page starts with scale: at least 200 proofs are known" and the on-screen metric label "Proofs named by the page" give catalog breadth evidentiary weight, even though the notes treat that count as atmosphere rather than support.
critiques.fix: Either relabel that count as breadth only and subordinate it, or cut it and spend the beat on quantities that reason from the notes: one guaranteed new prime, the factorial corollary, Bertrand's 2n window, and pi(x) ~ x / log x.

critiques.problem: "First compress the proposed list into one product" through "Either branch lands at the same destination" is a second happy-path retelling of the same flow the prior scene already established, so the film tours the mechanism twice instead of pressing where it fails as a generator.
critiques.fix: Repurpose this scene into the hard boundary from the notes: ask the theorem for the next prime, walk the composite-q case, and show that the proof yields only some prime factor outside the list, not a location or a next-prime recipe.

critiques.problem: "From any finite list, he guarantees 1 more prime outside it" jumps straight to Bertrand and the prime number theorem, but the notes keep one missing step in scope: Euclid's immediate factorial variation that proves for every positive integer n there is a prime bigger than n.
critiques.fix: Insert the factorial bridge before the stronger results: form n! + 1, derive that some prime factor exceeds n, and then say why Bertrand is stronger because it locates a prime in (n, 2n) instead of merely somewhere above n.

summary: This draft finds the theorem's real hinge and it does name a genuine trade-off, so it is not a flat admiration piece. But it still falls short of interrogation because the evidence hierarchy from the notes never becomes visible on screen, and the dedicated numbers material leans on catalog breadth while dropping the factorial bridge that would sharpen what Euclid does and does not prove.
